title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an urban beekeeper called Olivia Bennett talking about her work and the importance of bees in city environments. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an urban beekeeper called Olivia Bennett talking about her work and the importance of bees in city environments. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Olivia Bennett discusses the logistics and environmental benefits of keeping bees in London.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi everyone, I’m Olivia. When people think of London, they usually think of skyscrapers and traffic, not honeybees. But for the past five years, I’ve been running a series of hives on rooftops across the city. My journey into apiculture didn't start in a rural field; it actually began when I attended a [7]rooftop garden party[/7] at a friend’s office. I saw a small hive tucked away in a corner and was fascinated by how calm the bees were despite the noise of the city below. From that moment on, I was hooked.

Setting up an urban apiary is quite different from keeping bees in the countryside. You have to consider wind tunnels created by tall buildings and, of course, the heat island effect. Interestingly, I found that the [8]average temperature[/8] in the city allows bees to remain active for several weeks longer than their rural cousins. This means they can gather more nectar, but it also means the beekeeper has to be more vigilant about their food stores during the winter months.

One of the most common questions I get is what the bees actually eat in a concrete jungle. You’d be surprised by the variety. City bees often have a more diverse diet than those in agricultural areas because of the wide range of exotic plants in parks and private balconies. I recently did a chemical analysis of our summer honey and found that [9]lime trees[/9] provide the primary source of nectar for our Central London hives. The resulting honey has a distinct, almost minty aftertaste that our customers absolutely love.

However, the job isn't all about harvesting honey. A large part of my week is spent on 'swarm control'. In a crowded city, you can't have twenty thousand bees suddenly deciding to move into a neighbor’s chimney. To prevent this, I have to perform a [10]weekly inspection[/10] of every single hive during the spring. It’s physically demanding work, especially when you’re carrying heavy equipment up five flights of stairs in a full bee suit during a heatwave.

I’ve also become very involved in local education. I believe that understanding these insects is the key to protecting them. I was particularly pleased when a local primary school asked me to help them install a 'bee hotel'. I noticed that the children were most interested in the [11]magnifying glass[/11] I brought along, which allowed them to see the intricate structures of the bees' wings and legs. Seeing their fear turn into wonder is incredibly rewarding.

In terms of business, we’ve had to be creative. We don't just sell honey; we also use the leftover wax. I’ve found that our [12]handmade candles[/12] are actually our best-selling product during the Christmas season. They have a natural, subtle scent that you just can't replicate with synthetic paraffin. It’s a great way to ensure that absolutely nothing from the hive goes to waste.

Looking back, the biggest hurdle wasn't the bees themselves, but the [13]legal permits[/13] required to keep livestock on commercial buildings. It took nearly a year of negotiations with the city council to prove that our project was safe and beneficial for the local ecosystem. It was a frustrating process, but it paved the way for many other urban farmers.

Next year, we’re planning to expand even further. We’ve just received funding to launch a [14]community apprenticeship[/14] scheme for unemployed youth in East London. We want to teach them the skills of sustainable beekeeping and help them start their own small businesses. It’s about more than just bees; it’s about making the city a greener, more connected place to live. Thanks for listening.
